title: practical english usage - Part 285
content:
•  (k)nee (k)nife (k)nob (k)noek (k)now •  (h)onest (h)onour (h)our an unvoiced /w/  in these words.) and similarly in other words beginning wha, whe or whi. (Some speakers use •  w(h)at w(h)en w(h)ere w(h)ether w(h)ieh w(h)ip w(h)y Word  Formation and SpellingSection 30 vocabulary •  351  spelling and pronunciation r is pronounced whenever it is written.

In most varieties of American English, and in many regional British accents, India and Africa /'ɪndɪər ənd 'æfrɪkə/ written with r, if another vowel follows immediately.

We   often add /r/ after words ending in the sound /ə/   even when this is not theatre /'θɪətə(r)/ (AmE /'θi:ətər/) centre /'sentə(r)/                                  bothered /'boðəd/ iron /aɪən/  (AmE /'aɪərn/)                    wondered /'wʌndəd/ Note the pronunciation of iron, and of words ending in -re and -ered.

more eggs /'mɔ:r 'egz/ four islands /'fɔ:r 'aɪləndz/ But r is pronounced at the end of a word if a vowel follows immediately.

car /k a:/           four /fɔ:/          more /mɔ:/ hard /hɑ:d/        first /fɜ:st/       order /'ɔ:də/ consonant or at the end of a word.

In standard southern British English, r is not normally pronounced before a 15  silent     r friend /frend/          Worcester /'wustə/ foreign /'forən/                                women /'wɪmɪn/ Europe /’ joərəp/                              woman /'wumɔn/ Edinburgh /'edinbrə/                      two /tu:/ doesn't /'dʌz(ə)nt/                          queue /kju:/ does /dʌz/                                          one /wʌn/ clothes /kləoðz/                                  once /wʌns/ busy /'bɪzi/                                          (AmE  mustache /'mʌstæʃ,   mə'stæʃ/) business /'bɪzms/                            moustache /mə'stɑ:ʃ/ brooch /brəutʃ/                               minute /'minɪt/ blood /blʌd/                                      Leicester /'lestə/ biscuit /'bɪskɪ  t/                               Juice /dʒu:s/ bicycle /'baɪsɪkl/                              heart /hɑ:t/ ds Australia /    'treɪliə/                       heard /hɜ:d/ area /'eəriə/                                    fruit /fru:t/ 14 other     strange      spellings society either (some British speakers)   neither (some British speakers) biology buy dial height idea iron microphone science 13 words     pronounced         with    /aɪ/ put should would bull bullet bush butcher could cushion full pull push 12 u  or  ou    = /ʊ / trouble young country couple cousin double enough rough tough a =  /  / 11   ou vocabulary •  Section 31   Word  Problems from A  to Z ©     When  he laughs so, I want to scream. > 582.2 ©     I’ m  hot.
